terms.definition: Collective name for high-dimensional sparsity, distance concentration, and exploding sample complexity that make learning harder as feature dimension d grows. Canonical treatment in this topic.
With fixed sample size n, points occupy a vanishing fraction of feature-space volume as d increases, so local neighborhoods contain few informative training examples.
In high d, pairwise distances to a query concentrate near a common scale, shrinking relative gaps between nearest and farther points and weakening distance-based rankings.
Number of observations required to achieve a target density or estimation error; for grid-like coverage over d axes at resolution r, cell count grows as r^d.
High-dimensional phenomenon where a few points appear disproportionately often as nearest neighbors because distance ties and rankings become unstable.
Informal label for the fact that most volume of a high-dimensional ball or hypercube lies near its boundary, leaving interior regions sparsely populated by random samples.
